output: Yes, Orcish Bowmasters is one of the best creatures in Legacy. For 1B at instant speed, you get a 1/1 that punishes every cantrip, Brainstorm, and draw trigger your opponent makes. It creates amass tokens that grow quickly, acts as removal against small creatures, and provides a persistent clock. The card is efficient, disruptive, and resilient enough to be a multi-deck staple.

Yes, Murktide Regent is a Legacy staple. It frequently enters as a 6/6 or larger flyer for just UU by delving away graveyard fuel from cantrips and fetchlands. It dodges Lightning Bolt and Unholy Heat (when delirium is off), closes the game in 2-3 attacks, and pairs perfectly with the blue cantrip shell. Its mana efficiency relative to its stats makes it one of the best threats in the format.

Yes, Brainstorm is the most-played card in Legacy and arguably the format's defining spell. For a single blue mana at instant speed, it draws three cards and puts two back, which combined with fetchlands lets you shuffle away unwanted cards. This provides unmatched card selection and effectively turns every game into a curated draw. It's a 4-of in virtually every blue deck.

Yes, Force of Will is the backbone of Legacy's interaction. It can counter any spell for zero mana by exiling a blue card and paying 1 life, which is essential for keeping combo decks in check on turns 1 and 2. Without Force of Will, Legacy's fast combo decks would dominate. It's played as a 4-of in nearly every blue deck and is one of the format's most iconic cards.

Yes, Swords to Plowshares is the gold standard for creature removal in Legacy. For a single white mana at instant speed, it exiles any creature unconditionally. Exile is critical in a format with Uro, Murktide Regent, and various reanimation targets. The life gain drawback is negligible in a format where tempo and board control matter far more than life totals.

Yes, Lightning Bolt is Legacy-playable and sees regular play in red decks. One mana for 3 damage at instant speed is the benchmark for burn efficiency. It kills most relevant creatures including Bowmasters tokens, DRC, and Thalia, while also providing reach to close games. It's a staple in Izzet Delver, Burn, and various red midrange strategies.

Yes, Wasteland is a Legacy staple and one of the format's defining cards. It destroys any nonbasic land at no mana cost beyond sacrificing itself, which is devastating in a format built on duals, fetches, and utility lands. It enables mana denial strategies, punishes greedy manabases, and pairs beautifully with Rishadan Port and Life from the Loam. Nearly every non-combo deck plays it.

Yes, Delver of Secrets is a Legacy staple and the namesake of one of the format's most enduring archetypes. A 1-mana 3/2 flyer is an incredible rate when you can reliably flip it, and Legacy's cantrip-heavy blue decks do exactly that. It provides an early clock that pairs with countermagic and removal to create the classic tempo gameplan that has defined Legacy for over a decade.

Yes, Thoughtseize is highly Legacy-playable. For one black mana you see your opponent's entire hand and strip their best card, regardless of type. The 2 life cost is trivial. It's essential for combo decks needing to clear the way, and valuable in midrange and control for disrupting opposing gameplans. It's a staple in Reanimator, Death's Shadow, Scam, and other black strategies.

Yes, Aether Vial is Legacy-playable in specific tribal and creature-heavy strategies. It lets you deploy creatures at instant speed without paying mana, dodging countermagic entirely. It's a core card in Death & Taxes, Goblins, and Merfolk. The card is only strong in decks built around it, but in those shells it's one of the most powerful permanents in the format.

Yes, Dark Ritual is a Legacy staple in combo and fast-mana strategies. Adding BB for one mana enables explosive turn-1 plays like Necropotence, Entomb into Reanimate, or Ad Nauseam setups. It's a critical enabler in Storm, Reanimator, and various other combo shells. The raw mana efficiency is unmatched for enabling early kills.

Yes, Daze is Legacy-playable as a free counterspell in tempo strategies. Returning an Island to counter a spell when tapped out is extremely powerful in the early game, protecting threats like Delver or disrupting an opponent's curve. It loses value as the game goes long, but in aggressive blue decks the tempo advantage it provides in the first few turns is well worth the card.

instruction: Is Chalice of the Void Legacy-playable?
output: Yes, Chalice of the Void is a powerful Legacy card, especially on 1 where it blanks Brainstorm, Ponder, Swords to Plowshares, and most cantrips. It's a pillar of Stompy strategies like 8-Cast, Moon Stompy, and Eldrazi. Decks built to deploy it off Sol lands or Chrome Mox can lock opponents out of the game before they establish anything.

instruction: Is Thalia, Guardian of Thraben Legacy-playable?
output: Yes, Thalia is a Legacy staple in Death & Taxes and other white creature strategies. A 2/1 first strike for 1W that taxes all noncreature spells is brutal against cantrip-heavy blue decks, Storm, and combo. She effectively turns Brainstorm into a 2-mana spell and makes combo turns much harder to execute. She's one of the best hatebears ever printed.

Yes, Kappa Cannoneer is a Legacy staple in artifact-heavy decks like 8-Cast. Ward 4 makes it extremely difficult to remove, it grows with every artifact entering play, and it has built-in evasion. Combined with cheap artifacts and Urza's Saga tokens, it becomes a massive unblockable threat very quickly. It's one of the best payoffs for artifact strategies in the format.

Yes, Grief is Legacy-playable, particularly in Scam strategies that pair it with Reanimate or Not Dead After All. Evoking Grief on turn 1 to strip a card, then reanimating it to strip another while leaving a 3/2 menace body is a devastating opening. Even hard-cast, a 3/2 menace with an ETB Thoughtseize effect for 2BB is reasonable. The card has been a major player in black-based tempo.

Yes, Prismatic Ending is Legacy-playable as flexible removal in white-based multicolor decks. It exiles any nonland permanent with mana value X or less, making it effective against everything from Aether Vial to Sylvan Library to Bowmasters. The exile clause is crucial, and converge is easy to hit in a format with fetchlands and dual lands. It's a staple in Jeskai Control and 4-color piles.

instruction: Is Uro, Titan of Nature's Wrath Legacy-playable?
output: Yes, Uro is Legacy-playable and a powerful midrange threat. It gains 3 life, draws a card, and ramps on ETB, then escapes as a 6/6 that repeats this effect on attack. In a format full of fetchlands and cantrips filling the graveyard, escaping Uro is trivial. It provides card advantage, lifegain against aggro, and a resilient win condition all in one card.

Yes, Expressive Iteration is Legacy-playable as efficient card advantage in UR decks. For 1R at sorcery speed, you look at three cards, draw one, and play another this turn. In practice it's often a 2-mana draw-two, which is an excellent rate. It fuels Delver and Murktide strategies by finding both threats and answers. It's a staple in Izzet-based tempo and midrange builds.

Yes, Monastery Swiftspear is Legacy-playable in aggressive red strategies. A 1/2 haste with prowess for R provides an immediate clock that grows with every cantrip and burn spell. In Burn and UR Prowess shells, it frequently attacks for 3-4 damage on turn 2. The haste is critical for getting under countermagic-based defenses.

No, Siege Rhino is not Legacy-playable. At 4 mana in three colors (1WBG), it's far too slow and demanding for what it offers. A 4/5 trample that drains 3 on ETB is a fine rate in Standard but in Legacy you need your 4-drops to either win the game immediately or provide overwhelming card advantage. It also has no protection from removal, making it a poor investment at that cost.

Mostly no. In general Legacy, a 1/1 mana dork for G is too fragile since it dies to Orcish Bowmasters and Lightning Bolt without generating value. The one exception is the Elves combo deck, where tribal synergy with Heritage Druid, Glimpse of Nature, and Craterhoof Behemoth makes every elf body matter. Outside of that specific shell, Llanowar Elves is outclassed.

No, Baneslayer Angel is not Legacy-playable. At 5 mana for a creature with no ETB effect and no protection from countermagic or removal, it fails the fundamental test of Legacy threats. Compare it to Murktide Regent, which costs UU and is often bigger, or Atraxa, which draws four cards when reanimated. Baneslayer does nothing if it eats a Swords to Plowshares.

No, Sphinx's Revelation is not Legacy-playable. While drawing X cards and gaining X life sounds powerful, a sorcery-speed-like spell that requires XWUU is far too expensive. Legacy games are often decided by turns 3-4, and tapping out for a big Revelation is a death sentence against combo or tempo. Blue decks have Brainstorm, Ponder, and other efficient draw that don't require such investment.

No, Doom Blade is not Legacy-playable. While 1B to destroy a creature is a reasonable rate, the 'nonblack' restriction is a real cost when Bowmasters, Grief, and Sheoldred are everywhere. Fatal Push and Swords to Plowshares both outclass it by either costing less or having no restrictions. Legacy removal needs to be either cheaper or more flexible.

Yes, Counterspell is Legacy-playable but not universally played. UU for an unconditional hard counter is a solid rate, and it sees play in control decks that can afford to hold up two mana. However, many tempo decks prefer Daze and Force of Will for their free casting, so Counterspell tends to appear more in Miracles and control builds than in Delver shells.

Yes, Mox Diamond is a Legacy staple in Lands and various prison strategies. Discarding a land to get a 0-mana rock is powerful when you have excess lands or want to enable fast Exploration, Chalice, or Trinisphere. In Lands specifically, the discarded land often fuels Life from the Loam. It's a key accelerant that lets fair decks keep pace with combo's speed.

No, Hero's Downfall is not Legacy-playable. At 1BB for an instant-speed destroy effect, it's simply too expensive when Swords to Plowshares costs W and exiles, or Fatal Push costs B with revolt. The ability to hit planeswalkers is rarely relevant enough to justify the extra two mana. Legacy demands ruthless mana efficiency from its removal.

Yes, True-Name Nemesis is Legacy-playable, though less dominant than it once was. A 3/1 with protection from a chosen player is effectively unblockable and nearly unremovable by targeted effects. It's still strong in fair blue decks and with equipment. However, Bowmasters' ping effect and various -X/-X effects have made it more vulnerable than in years past.

Yes, Snapcaster Mage is Legacy-playable in control and midrange shells. A 2/1 flash for 1U that gives a spell in your graveyard flashback provides instant-speed interaction and card advantage. Flashing back Brainstorm, Swords to Plowshares, or a counterspell is excellent. It's less common in tempo due to the 2-mana cost but remains strong in Jeskai and Grixis Control.

Tarmogoyf is still technically Legacy-playable but has fallen out of favor. A 2-mana creature that's often a 4/5 or 5/6 was once dominant, but it lacks evasion, protection, and ETB value. Murktide Regent, Bowmasters, and Barrowgoyf have all pushed Tarmogoyf to the margins. It's still acceptable in some Temur or BG shells, but it's no longer the default green threat.

No, Collected Company is generally not Legacy-playable. At 4 mana, it competes with much more powerful options, and many Legacy creatures have mana values above 3 (Murktide, Uro) or are better cheated in via other means. The hit rate in most Legacy decks is unreliable. While fringe creature-combo decks have tried it, the card has never found a consistent home in the format.

Yes, Rest in Peace is Legacy-playable as a powerful sideboard card. For 1W it exiles all graveyards and prevents anything from entering them, shutting down Reanimator, Dredge, Uro escape, Murktide delve, and DRC delirium. It's one of the cleanest graveyard hate options available and a common 2-of in white sideboards across the format.

No, Opt is not Legacy-playable. While a 1-mana instant cantrip is fine in Standard, Legacy has Brainstorm, Ponder, and Preordain, which all provide vastly superior card selection. Scry 1 then draw 1 is simply too weak when you could be seeing three cards with Brainstorm or arranging your top three with Ponder. There's no deck that wants Opt over these alternatives.

Yes, Stoneforge Mystic is a Legacy staple. For 1W, it tutors an equipment on ETB and can cheat it into play for 1W at instant speed. Fetching Kaldra Compleat or Batterskull and deploying it on turn 3 at instant speed is one of the most efficient threat packages in the format. It's the backbone of Death & Taxes and various Stoneblade strategies.

instruction: Is Jace, the Mind Sculptor Legacy-playable?
output: Yes, Jace, the Mind Sculptor is Legacy-playable in control strategies. His Brainstorm ability provides repeatable card advantage, the bounce handles creatures, and the fateseal locks opponents out. At 4 mana he's on the expensive side for Legacy, so he fits best in Miracles and control builds that can protect him. He's less dominant than he once was but still a powerful finisher.

No, Negate is generally not Legacy-playable in maindecks. While countering noncreature spells for 1U is fine, Legacy decks prefer Force of Will (free), Daze (free), and Counterspell (hits everything). Negate occasionally appears in sideboards for combo matchups, but even there Flusterstorm is usually preferred since it costs U and is uncounterable via storm copies.

instruction: Is Emrakul, the Aeons Torn Legacy-playable?
output: Yes, Emrakul is Legacy-playable as a reanimation and Show and Tell target. You never hard-cast it for 15; instead, you cheat it into play via Sneak Attack, Show and Tell, or occasionally reanimate it. Annihilator 6, protection from colored spells, and the extra turn on cast make it one of the most devastating threats when deployed. It's a staple finisher in Sneak and Show.

Vendilion Clique is marginally Legacy-playable but has fallen out of favor. A 3/1 flash flyer for 1UU that lets you strip a card from the opponent's hand was once a staple, but 3 mana is a lot for a 3/1, and it dies to Bowmasters' ping trigger. Endurance, Subtlety, and other flash threats have largely replaced it. It still occasionally shows up but is no longer a format staple.

Yes, Sylvan Library is a Legacy staple in green decks. For 1G, it lets you draw two extra cards per turn at the cost of 4 life each, or rearrange your top cards for free. Combined with fetchlands to shuffle away unwanted cards, it's a repeatable Brainstorm effect in green. It's a key card in Lands, Maverick, and various green midrange strategies.

No, Wrath of God is not Legacy-playable. At 4 mana for a sorcery-speed sweeper, it's too slow for the format. Terminus costs W with miracle and is instant-speed off a Brainstorm, which makes it strictly better in Legacy's cantrip-heavy blue decks. Even non-blue decks prefer targeted removal or Toxic Deluge, which costs 3 mana and gets around indestructible.

Yes, Toxic Deluge is Legacy-playable and the premier black sweeper. For 2B and X life, it gives all creatures -X/-X, dodging indestructible, regeneration, and protection. The life payment is a feature, not a bug, since you choose exactly how much to pay. It's efficient at 3 mana, kills True-Name Nemesis, and is a staple in black sideboards and Painter builds.

Yes, Abrupt Decay is Legacy-playable as uncounterable removal for permanents with mana value 3 or less. It hits Counterbalance, Aether Vial, Delver, Bowmasters, and most relevant threats. The 'can't be countered' clause is extremely valuable against blue decks. It's a staple in BG/x midrange strategies like Golgari Depths, BUG Zenith, and various Abzan builds.

No, Hydroid Krasis is not Legacy-playable. While it's a flexible creature that draws cards and gains life, the XGU cost means it requires a massive mana investment to be impactful. A 4/4 flyer that draws 2 for 6 mana is terrible by Legacy standards. Cards like Uro do the same job of gaining life and drawing cards for half the mana.

Yes, Dragon's Rage Channeler (DRC) is a Legacy staple. For R, it's a 1/1 that surveys on attack (filling your graveyard for delirium and delve) and becomes a 3/3 flyer with delirium active. In a format full of fetchlands, cantrips, and cheap spells, achieving delirium is trivial. It's a key threat in Izzet Delver alongside Murktide Regent and pairs perfectly with the cantrip shell.

Goblin Guide is marginally Legacy-playable, appearing in Burn and occasional aggressive red strategies. A 2/2 haste for R is an efficient clock, and the card-giving drawback matters less when your plan is to kill before those extra cards are relevant. However, it's outclassed by DRC in tempo decks and is only truly at home in dedicated Burn lists.

No, Questing Beast is not Legacy-playable. At 4 mana, its wall of text doesn't compensate for costing as much as Jace, the Mind Sculptor while being far easier to remove. It has no protection, no ETB value, and dies to every piece of removal in the format. Four-mana creatures in Legacy need to be game-ending or generate immediate card advantage.

Yes, Endurance is a Legacy staple. A 3/4 flash reach for 1GG is already a reasonable body, but the real value is its free evoke to shuffle an entire graveyard into a library at instant speed. It wrecks Reanimator, Dredge, and Murktide while also being castable as a solid blocker against Delver. It's played in maindecks and sideboards across green strategies.

instruction: Is Liliana of the Veil Legacy-playable?
output: Liliana of the Veil is technically Legacy-playable but has become much less common. Her +1 symmetric discard and -2 edict were strong in an era of single-threat decks, but modern Legacy decks go wide with tokens (Bowmasters, Forth Eorlingas!) making edicts weak, and many threats replace themselves on ETB. At 3 mana, she's been surpassed by more impactful options.

Yes, Forth Eorlingas! is Legacy-playable in red aggressive and midrange strategies. For XR it creates X 1/1 hasty Human tokens, and if any deal combat damage you become the monarch. Even for 2R making two tokens and drawing an extra card per turn is an excellent rate. The monarch mechanic provides persistent card advantage that's hard to reclaim in a tempo-focused format.

Yes, Preordain is Legacy-playable as the third-best cantrip after Brainstorm and Ponder. Scry 2 then draw 1 for U provides good card selection, especially in combo decks that need specific pieces. It's less common in Delver shells (which prefer the raw power of Brainstorm and Ponder) but sees play in Storm, Show and Tell, and other combo strategies that want additional filtering.

No, Elvish Mystic is not generally Legacy-playable for the same reasons as Llanowar Elves. A 1/1 mana dork dies to Bowmasters on your opponent's first draw trigger and to Lightning Bolt. The tempo loss of having your 1-drop killed for free is too punishing. The exception is Elves combo, where tribal synergy makes every elf body count regardless of individual fragility.

instruction: Is Minsc & Boo, Timeless Heroes Legacy-playable?
output: Yes, Minsc & Boo is Legacy-playable in midrange strategies. For 2RG, it provides a planeswalker that creates a trampling Hamster token, can fling it for direct damage, and threatens to ultimate. The combination of a recurring threat, reach via fling, and the ability to close games quickly makes it a potent top-end in Temur and RG-based midrange decks.

instruction: How would a 2-mana instant that draws 2 cards affect Legacy?
output: A 2-mana instant that draws 2 cards with no downside would almost certainly be banned in Legacy. Treasure Cruise was banned and it required delve fuel, while Ancestral Recall is restricted in Vintage for drawing 3 at 1 mana. A clean 2-mana draw-2 instant would be better than Expressive Iteration (which is sorcery and requires playing the second card immediately) and would slot into every blue deck. The format would warp around it overnight.

instruction: How would a 3-mana creature with flash, hexproof, and draws a card on ETB affect Legacy?
output: A 3-mana flash hexproof creature that draws a card on ETB would likely be playable depending on its stats. Compare it to Ice-Fang Coatl (2 mana, 1/1, flash, draws on ETB, conditional deathtouch) which sees play. At 3 mana with hexproof, it would need reasonable stats (at least 2/2) to matter. If it were a 3/2 or better, it would be a strong option for midrange and control as a threat that replaces itself and dodges targeted removal.

instruction: When Orcish Bowmasters was spoiled, what made it obviously Legacy-playable?
output: Several factors made Bowmasters obviously Legacy-playable on sight. First, flash at 2 mana is the right rate for the format. Second, it directly punishes Legacy's core mechanic: cantrips. Every Brainstorm, Ponder, and Sylvan Library trigger gives you a damage ping and grows your army token. Third, the amass token scales over time. Fourth, it doubles as creature removal against X/1s. It was clearly designed to prey on exactly what Legacy decks do most.

instruction: How would a 1-mana enchantment that says 'whenever an opponent draws a card, they lose 1 life' affect Legacy?
output: This would be very strong in Legacy and likely see widespread play. In a format where Brainstorm draws 3, Ponder effectively draws 1, and players cantrip multiple times per game, passive life loss adds up quickly. Compare to Bowmasters which requires 2 mana and a creature body. A 1-mana enchantment is harder to remove (dodges creature removal) and would punish blue decks severely. It might even push some cantrip-heavy strategies out of the meta.

instruction: How would a 0-mana artifact that enters tapped and taps for one mana of any color affect Legacy?
output: A 0-mana rock that enters tapped and taps for any color would be extremely powerful and likely banned. Even entering tapped, a free mana source enables devastating turn-1 plays: Chalice on 1, 2-drop creatures, or fuel for combo. It would slot into every Stompy deck, improve Storm, and make Ancient Tomb decks even more explosive. Free mana is the most dangerous thing in Legacy; Mox Diamond already requires a land discard and sees heavy play.

instruction: How would a 2-mana sorcery that says 'destroy target land' affect Legacy?
output: A 2-mana Sinkhole is already a Legacy card, and it's playable but not dominant. The effect is strong in Pox and land destruction strategies but not format-warping because it requires you to spend your turn 2 on mana denial rather than developing your board. Wasteland is more impactful because it's free (costs only the land itself) and doesn't cost a card from your spell slots.

instruction: How would a 1-mana blue instant that counters target creature spell affect Legacy?
output: A 1-mana unconditional creature counter would be solid but not format-breaking. Legacy already has Spell Pierce (conditional), Force of Will (free but 2-for-1), and various 2-mana counters. A dedicated creature-only counter for U would be a clean sideboard option against creature-heavy decks but too narrow for maindecks where you also need to counter noncreature spells. It would see play but not warp the format.

instruction: How would a 3-mana black enchantment that says 'at the beginning of each opponent's upkeep, that player discards a card' affect Legacy?
output: This would be playable but not dominant. Compare to Liliana of the Veil's +1 (symmetric discard each turn) or Chains of Mephistopheles (disrupts draws). At 3 mana for a single discard per turn, it's slow by Legacy standards. By turn 3, many opponents have already deployed their key threats or are topdecking anyway. It would be decent in Pox or 8-Rack strategies but wouldn't displace existing options.

instruction: How would a 2-mana white creature with 'when this enters, exile target nonland permanent with mana value 2 or less' affect Legacy?
output: This would be very strong in Legacy. A 2-mana creature with an ETB Prismatic Ending for 2 or less hits Bowmasters, DRC, Delver, Aether Vial, Sylvan Library, and countless other targets. The body provides additional value for flickering or attacking. It would likely become a staple in Death & Taxes and white midrange decks, potentially even splashed in other strategies. The permanent exile on a body is extremely efficient.

instruction: How would a free counterspell that only works on your own turn affect Legacy?
output: A free counterspell that only works on your own turn would be interesting but niche. It would primarily protect your own combo turns from disruption, making it strong in Storm, Show and Tell, and other combo decks that need to resolve key spells through Force of Will. However, it wouldn't help against opposing threats on their turn, limiting its versatility. It would be a powerful but specialized combo protection tool, likely a 2-of in sideboards.

instruction: How would a 1-mana green instant that says 'target creature you control gains hexproof and indestructible until end of turn, draw a card' affect Legacy?
output: This would be format-warping and almost certainly too strong. A 1-mana cantripping protection spell is already excellent (compare Veil of Summer, which is banned in some formats). Adding both hexproof and indestructible means it blanks every removal spell in the format while replacing itself. Every green creature deck would play 4 copies. It would make threats like Tarmogoyf and Uro nearly impossible to remove efficiently.

instruction: How would a 2-mana land that taps for any color and can't be destroyed by nonland sources affect Legacy?
output: A 2-mana land with any color and immunity to nonland destruction would be very strong in multicolor decks. It still dies to Wasteland (which is a land source) but dodges Vindicate, Abrupt Decay, and similar effects. The real question is whether paying 2 mana to play a land is worth the fixing. In Legacy, you have free dual lands and fetches, so a 2-mana investment for a land is probably too slow for most strategies. It would be niche at best.

instruction: How would a 1-mana black instant that says 'look at target opponent's hand, choose a nonland card, exile it' affect Legacy?
output: This is essentially Thoughtseize with exile instead of discard, which is a meaningful upgrade in Legacy. Exiling prevents Uro escaping, stops Kozilek's shuffle triggers, and prevents any graveyard recursion. Without the 2 life cost of Thoughtseize, it would be strictly better and would likely see play as a 4-of in every black deck. With a life cost, it would be a sidecar or replacement for Thoughtseize depending on the metagame.

instruction: When Wrenn and Six was first printed, what made it Legacy-playable?
output: Wrenn and Six was obviously Legacy-playable because it turned Wasteland into a repeatable lock. For just RG, you get a planeswalker that recurs lands every turn, meaning you could Wasteland your opponent every single turn while never missing land drops. It also pinged X/1 creatures, handled DRC and other small threats, and its -1 was incidentally useful. It was so dominant it was banned in Legacy for enabling oppressive Wasteland recursion.

instruction: How would a 2-mana artifact that says 'spells your opponents cast cost 1 more' affect Legacy?
output: A 2-mana Sphere of Resistance already exists and is fringe playable. Thorn of Amethyst does this for noncreature spells specifically and sees play in Stompy. A clean 2-mana tax on all spells would be strong in prison and Stompy strategies, especially since it stacks with Thalia and Trinisphere. It would be a staple in Moon Stompy and Eldrazi shells but not format-warping since artifact removal handles it.

instruction: How would a 1-mana instant that says 'counter target spell if its controller has more cards in hand than you' affect Legacy?
output: This would be a fascinating addition to Legacy. In Delver and tempo strategies that aggressively spend cards, you'd frequently have fewer cards in hand, making this a 1-mana hard counter. Against control decks that hold up cards, it becomes excellent. However, it's dead when you're the one with more cards, making it unreliable in control mirrors. It would be a strong tempo card, probably a 2-3 of in Delver sideboards, but too conditional for maindecks.

instruction: How would a 3-mana planeswalker with '+1: draw a card, -2: destroy target creature, starting loyalty 3' affect Legacy?
output: This would be very strong and likely a multi-deck staple. Compare to Jace (4 mana, Brainstorm but no removal) and Liliana of the Veil (3 mana, edict but no card draw). A 3-mana walker that both draws and removes creatures is exactly what midrange and control want. Starting at 3 loyalty means it survives a bolt after using +1. The color identity would determine which decks play it, but the rate is clearly Legacy-caliber.

instruction: How would a 0-mana creature with 'exile two cards from your graveyard: this creature gains flying until end of turn, 2/1 stats' affect Legacy?
output: A 0-mana 2/1 would be extremely powerful in Legacy regardless of additional text. Free creatures break fundamental rules of Magic. Compare Memnite (0-mana 1/1) which is niche, but a 2/1 with upside would be maindeckable. It would slot into Affinity, aggro, and could be pitched to various force effects. The flying activation with graveyard fuel is gravy. It would likely need banning as free threats with relevant stats historically break formats.

instruction: When Minsc & Boo was printed, what signaled it could be Legacy-playable?
output: Minsc & Boo showed Legacy potential because of its immediate board impact and versatility. Creating a hasty trampling token on ETB means you get value even if the planeswalker is immediately removed. The fling ability provides reach that bypasses blockers and creates a clock opponents must respect. At 4 mana it's expensive for Legacy, but the combination of a threat, removal, and reach in one card justified the cost in midrange Temur shells.

instruction: How would a land that taps for colorless and has 'tap, sacrifice: counter target activated or triggered ability' affect Legacy?
output: This would be a strong utility land in Legacy. Stifle effects on a land are valuable because they can't be countered by Force of Will and don't cost a card slot. It would counter fetchland activations, Wasteland triggers, Bowmasters triggers, and various combo abilities. Compare to Stifle itself which sees play. The sacrifice cost is real but acceptable for a free Stifle effect. It would likely see play in Lands and various colorless-friendly decks.

instruction: Compare Force of Will vs Force of Negation in Legacy.
output: Force of Will is far superior in Legacy. FoW counters any spell at any time by exiling a blue card and paying 1 life, making it the format's essential safety valve against combo on any turn. Force of Negation only works on noncreature spells and only during opponents' turns (when cast for free), meaning it can't protect your own combo turn or stop creatures. FoN is a supplementary piece in some sideboards, while FoW is a universal 4-of staple.

instruction: Compare Brainstorm vs Ponder in Legacy.
output: Brainstorm is better than Ponder in Legacy because fetchlands make it extraordinary. Brainstorm draws 3 and puts 2 back, then a fetchland shuffles away the unwanted cards, effectively making it a 1-mana draw-3. Ponder looks at 3 and draws 1, with an optional shuffle. Without fetchlands, Ponder is arguably better since Brainstorm can lock you with bad cards on top. But Legacy runs 4+ fetches, so Brainstorm is the clear king. Both are 4-ofs in most blue decks.

instruction: Compare Tarmogoyf vs Barrowgoyf in Legacy.
output: Barrowgoyf is generally better than Tarmogoyf in current Legacy. Both cost 2 mana and scale with card types in graveyards, but Barrowgoyf has menace, making it harder to block, and its adapt ability provides late-game value. Tarmogoyf is a vanilla beater that gets stonewalled by tokens and chump blockers. The menace evasion on Barrowgoyf is a significant upgrade in a format full of small blockers and token generation.

instruction: Compare Wasteland vs Ghost Quarter in Legacy.
output: Wasteland is far superior to Ghost Quarter in Legacy. Both destroy nonbasic lands, but Wasteland doesn't give the opponent anything, while Ghost Quarter lets them search for a basic. In a format where mana denial is a core strategy, giving the opponent a replacement land completely undermines the purpose. Wasteland creates true tempo advantage and can strand opponents off colors, while Ghost Quarter merely fixes their mana. Wasteland is a 4-of staple; Ghost Quarter sees almost no play.

instruction: Compare Swords to Plowshares vs Fatal Push in Legacy.
output: Both are elite removal but serve different roles. Swords to Plowshares costs W, exiles any creature unconditionally, and the life gain is negligible downside. Fatal Push costs B, destroys creatures with mana value 2 or less (4 or less with revolt). StP is more versatile since it hits everything from Murktide to Emrakul, while Push is limited to small creatures without revolt. StP is the format's best removal spell; Push is excellent but more situational.

instruction: Compare Ponder vs Preordain in Legacy.
output: Ponder is better than Preordain in Legacy. Ponder sees 3 cards and offers a shuffle if none are good, while Preordain scries 2 and draws 1. Ponder's ability to see 3 and then shuffle for a fresh random card gives it more raw selection power. However, Preordain is better at putting unwanted cards on the bottom permanently. In practice, Ponder is a 4-of in almost every blue deck, while Preordain is the 5th-8th cantrip for combo decks that want extra filtering.

instruction: Compare Dark Ritual vs Lotus Petal in Legacy.
output: Both provide fast mana but serve different purposes. Dark Ritual gives BB for B (net +2 black), while Lotus Petal gives 1 mana of any color for free but costs a card. Ritual is better in mono-black combo like Storm or Reanimator where you need multiple black mana in sequence. Petal is better in multicolor combo or when you need mana without having any lands. Storm decks often play both, using Petal for initial mana and Ritual for explosive sequencing.

instruction: Compare Terminus vs Toxic Deluge in Legacy.
output: Both are premium sweepers but for different decks. Terminus costs W with miracle (set up via Brainstorm) and puts creatures on the bottom, dodging death triggers and indestructible. Toxic Deluge costs 2B plus life and gives -X/-X, beating indestructible and protection. Terminus is better in blue cantrip decks that can reliably miracle it. Deluge is better in non-blue decks and against True-Name Nemesis. Terminus goes in Miracles; Deluge goes in black midrange.

instruction: Compare Show and Tell vs Reanimate as cheating strategies in Legacy.
output: Both cheat large threats into play but with different tradeoffs. Show and Tell costs 2U and lets both players put any permanent into play, risking the opponent dropping something equally threatening. Reanimate costs B and puts a creature from any graveyard into play at the cost of life equal to its mana value, but requires graveyard setup. Reanimate is faster (turn 1 possible) but more vulnerable to graveyard hate. Show and Tell is slower but dodges graveyard hate.

instruction: Compare Karakas vs Cavern of Souls in Legacy.
output: These serve completely different purposes. Karakas is a legendary land that bounces legendary creatures, acting as repeatable removal against opposing legends (Uro, Emrakul) and protection for your own. Cavern of Souls makes a chosen creature type uncounterable, crucial for tribal decks against blue. Karakas is more universally useful as both offense and defense in a format full of legends. Cavern is narrower but essential in Goblins, Elves, and similar tribal strategies.

instruction: Compare Hymn to Tourach vs Thoughtseize in Legacy.
output: Thoughtseize (B, targeted, 2 life) is generally more reliable since you choose the best card to take. Hymn to Tourach (BB, random 2 cards) has higher ceiling but lower floor, sometimes hitting 2 lands and sometimes hitting their best 2 spells. Hymn's randomness can be devastating, and taking 2 cards for 2 mana is incredible rate. Thoughtseize is played more widely because it's cheaper, targeted, and splashable. Hymn is strong in dedicated black aggro-discard shells.

instruction: Compare Volcanic Island vs Steam Vents in Legacy.
output: Volcanic Island is strictly better in Legacy. It's an untapped UR dual land with basic land types that can be fetched, just like Steam Vents, but it never costs you 2 life. In a format with Wasteland, both die equally, but Volcanic Island preserves your life total which matters against aggressive decks and Death's Shadow. Steam Vents sees zero Legacy play because the original duals exist and have no downside.

instruction: Compare Murktide Regent vs Tombstalker in Legacy.
output: Murktide Regent is vastly superior to Tombstalker. Both use delve to cheat on mana costs, but Murktide costs UU (typically 2 total mana) while Tombstalker costs BB with a higher base cost of 6BB. Murktide also grows from exiled cards and is often a 6/6 to 8/8 flyer, while Tombstalker is always a 5/5. Murktide's UU cost fits perfectly in cantrip-heavy blue shells. Tombstalker has been completely obsoleted.

instruction: Compare Rishadan Port vs Wasteland in Legacy.
output: Both deny mana but differently. Wasteland permanently destroys a nonbasic land at the cost of sacrificing itself. Rishadan Port taps a land for a turn for 1 mana, repeatable but temporary. Wasteland is better for one-shot tempo blowouts and killing utility lands forever. Port is better for sustained pressure, tapping their land each upkeep while you keep yours. Many D&T and Stompy lists play both for maximum mana denial. They complement rather than compete.

instruction: Compare Entomb vs Buried Alive in Legacy Reanimator.
output: Entomb is far superior in Legacy Reanimator. At B and instant speed, it enables turn-1 kills with Dark Ritual into Entomb plus Reanimate. Buried Alive costs 2B (sorcery speed) and puts three creatures in the graveyard, which is overkill when you only need one reanimation target. The 2 extra mana on Buried Alive means it's essentially unplayable in a format where speed is everything. Entomb is a 4-of staple; Buried Alive sees no competitive play.

instruction: Compare Retrofitter Foundry vs Urza's Saga in Legacy.
output: These are different cards that often work together. Urza's Saga is a land that creates construct tokens and tutors for 0-1 mana artifacts, including Retrofitter Foundry. Foundry is a 1-mana artifact that converts tokens into progressively larger ones (1/1 servo to 1/1 thopter to 4/4 construct). Saga is more universally powerful as a land that generates threats and tutors. Foundry is a value engine that Saga can find. Saga is the staple; Foundry is the synergy piece.

instruction: Compare Flusterstorm vs Spell Pierce in Legacy.
output: Both are 1-mana blue counterspells but with different strengths. Flusterstorm targets instants/sorceries with storm, making it devastating against Storm combo where each copy must be paid for individually. Spell Pierce targets noncreature spells and is beaten by paying 2. Flusterstorm is better in counter wars and against combo. Spell Pierce is more versatile, hitting artifacts, enchantments, and planeswalkers too. Flusterstorm is more common in sideboards; Spell Pierce appears in maindecks.

instruction: Compare Narset, Parter of Veils vs Leovold, Emissary of Trest in Legacy.
output: Both restrict opponents to one draw per turn, but Leovold is stronger overall. Leovold is a 3/3 body for BUG that also draws you a card whenever opponents target your permanents, making him a threat and protection rolled into one. Narset is a 3-mana planeswalker that digs for noncreature spells but has no body. Leovold's creature type makes him easier to remove but also means he can attack. Leovold is the premier choice when you have access to all three colors.

instruction: Compare Ancient Tomb vs City of Traitors in Legacy.
output: Both provide 2 colorless mana but with different costs. Ancient Tomb deals 2 damage per tap but stays in play permanently. City of Traitors is sacrificed when you play another land. Ancient Tomb is generally better because it persists and the 2 life per use is manageable. City is better when you only need the burst for one turn. Stompy decks often play both for maximum fast-mana density, but Ancient Tomb is the more important of the two.

instruction: Compare Carpet of Flowers vs Veil of Summer as anti-blue tech in Legacy.
output: Both fight blue decks but differently. Carpet of Flowers (G enchantment) generates mana equal to opponent's Islands each upkeep, providing massive sustained ramp against blue. Veil of Summer (G instant) gives one turn of hexproof from blue/black and draws a card if they've cast a blue/black spell. Carpet is better in fair green decks that want to go over the top with mana advantage across many turns. Veil is better as combo protection for one critical turn.

instruction: Compare Chrome Mox vs Mox Diamond in Legacy.
output: Both are 0-mana accelerants with card disadvantage. Chrome Mox exiles a nonartifact, nonland card for its color. Mox Diamond discards a land for any color. Mox Diamond is more widely played because extra lands are easier to afford than extra spells, and it works with Life from the Loam to mitigate the discard. Chrome Mox is better in spell-heavy combo decks with redundant pieces. Mox Diamond is the staple in Lands and Stompy; Chrome Mox is more niche.

instruction: Compare Natural Order vs Green Sun's Zenith in Legacy.
output: Both tutor green creatures but with very different applications. Natural Order (2GG, sacrifice a green creature) puts any green creature directly into play, enabling turn-2 Craterhoof Behemoth in Elves. Green Sun's Zenith (XG, sorcery) searches for a creature with mana value X and shuffles back in. GSZ is more versatile since it scales from Dryad Arbor (X=0) to any green creature, while Natural Order has a higher ceiling but requires sacrifice fodder.

instruction: Compare The One Ring vs Sylvan Library as card advantage engines in Legacy.
output: Both provide powerful card advantage but in different shells. The One Ring (4 mana artifact) gives protection the turn it enters and draws increasing cards each turn at the cost of life. Sylvan Library (1G enchantment) draws 2 extra per turn for 4 life each. The One Ring is colorless and fits in any deck, but costs 4 and the cumulative life loss is steep. Sylvan Library is cheaper and more sustainable with fetchland shuffling. Library is better in green decks; The One Ring fits artifact strategies.

Yes, Glimpse of Nature is a Legacy staple specifically in the Elves combo deck. For a single green mana, it turns every creature you cast into a cantrip, enabling massive chain turns where you play 10+ elves and draw into Craterhoof Behemoth or Natural Order. Outside of Elves, it's unplayable since no other deck has the critical mass of cheap creatures to abuse it. It's the engine that makes Elves a viable combo deck.

Yes, Shark Typhoon is Legacy-playable in control decks. Its cycling ability creates an uncounterable flash flying token at instant speed, which is valuable in counter wars and against Cavern of Souls. As an enchantment, it also provides a secondary game plan of generating tokens from your normal spell casting. It's most common in Jeskai and UW Control as a 1-2 of finisher that never risks being stranded in hand.

Yorion is Legacy-playable as an 80-card companion in specific control and midrange shells. A 4/5 flyer for 3WU that blinks your permanents is decent, but the real value is having a guaranteed extra card as companion. The 80-card deckbuilding restriction is a real cost in Legacy where consistency is king, but decks with enough redundant cantrips and removal can absorb it. It sees play in D&T and 4-color control builds.

Deathrite Shaman was so Legacy-playable that it was banned in 2018. For B/G, it provided mana acceleration by exiling lands, graveyard hate by exiling instants/sorceries, and a life-draining clock by exiling creatures. A 1-mana card that functions as a Bird of Paradise, graveyard hate, and win condition simultaneously was format-warping. Every black or green deck played 4 copies, homogenizing the format.

Yes, Walking Ballista is Legacy-playable in specific strategies. It's a key combo piece in Heliod combo (infinite damage with Heliod, Sun-Crowned giving it lifelink) and a tutorable win condition off Urza's Saga for X=0-1. On its own, a scalable creature that can ping anything is flexible, though paying XX for an X/X is a poor rate. Its real value is as a combo piece and Saga target rather than as a standalone threat.

No, Smuggler's Copter is not competitively played in Legacy. While a 3/3 flyer for 2 that loots on attack is a fine rate, requiring a creature to crew it is a real cost in a format where your creatures are often killed on sight. Legacy decks have better options for card filtering (cantrips) and better threats (Murktide, Bowmasters). The vehicle type also lacks meaningful synergies in the format.

No, Maddening Hex is not Legacy-playable. A 3-mana enchantment that deals random damage when the enchanted player casts spells is too slow, too inconsistent, and too expensive for Legacy. The damage is random (1-6), the cost is high, and it doesn't affect the board immediately. Legacy requires cards that are efficient and reliable, not ones that rely on randomness and gradual damage.

Yes, Psychic Frog is Legacy-playable and has seen strong results in UB-based midrange and tempo strategies. For UB you get a creature that grows by discarding cards, can gain flying, and has a Shadowmage Infiltrator-style draw trigger. It synergizes with the graveyard-matters cards in Legacy, turns dead draws into power, and provides both a clock and card advantage. It's proven itself in Dimir and Grixis shells.

No, Etali, Primal Storm is not Legacy-playable. At 6 mana for a creature with no ETB effect, no protection, and an ability that only triggers on attack, it's far too slow and vulnerable. Compare to Atraxa (reanimated for 1-2 mana, draws 4 immediately) or Emrakul (cheated in with annihilator 6). Anything you're paying 6 mana for in Legacy needs to be immediately game-ending or come with built-in protection.

Yes, Retrofitter Foundry is Legacy-playable, primarily because Urza's Saga can tutor for it. For 1 mana, it provides a repeatable token-generation engine that converts servos into thopters into 4/4 constructs. In grindy matchups, it takes over the game by producing an endless stream of increasingly large threats. It's a key piece in 8-Cast and other Saga-based artifact strategies.

instruction: Is Omnath, Locus of Creation Legacy-playable?
output: Omnath, Locus of Creation is fringe Legacy-playable in 4-color midrange builds. Drawing a card on ETB is good, and gaining 4 life then making 4 mana from landfall triggers is powerful. However, requiring RGWU is extremely demanding, and at 4 mana it competes with other powerful options. It's playable when the mana supports it and fetchlands enable multiple landfall triggers, but it's not a format staple.

instruction: Compare Daze vs Force of Will in Legacy.
output: Both are free counterspells but serve different roles. Force of Will costs a card from hand (exiling a blue card plus 1 life) and works at any time against any spell. Daze returns an Island and works best in the early game when opponents are tapped out. Force is the format's safety valve against combo and is essential at all stages. Daze is a tempo tool that becomes dead in the late game. Force is the more important card, but Daze's early-game efficiency makes it a staple in tempo decks.

instruction: Compare Sneak Attack vs Show and Tell as enablers in Legacy.
output: Both cheat large creatures into play but differently. Show and Tell (2U sorcery) lets both players put a permanent into play, risking your opponent deploying something threatening. Sneak Attack (3R enchantment) gives creatures haste for R each but they're sacrificed at end of turn. Show and Tell is faster (turn 1-2 possible with acceleration) but symmetric. Sneak Attack is slower to deploy but one-sided and repeatable. Most Sneak and Show decks play both for redundancy.

instruction: Compare Stifle vs Dress Down as anti-ETB tools in Legacy.
output: Stifle (U instant) counters any activated or triggered ability but is narrow and requires precise timing. Dress Down (1U enchantment) removes all abilities from creatures for a turn and draws a card on sacrifice. Dress Down is broader, shutting down all creature abilities simultaneously and cantripping, but it's sorcery speed and only lasts a turn. Stifle is better for surgical disruption of fetchlands and specific triggers. Dress Down is better as a blanket creature-ability hoser.

instruction: Compare Mishra's Bauble vs Urza's Bauble in Legacy.
output: Mishra's Bauble is significantly better in Legacy. Both are 0-mana artifacts that draw a card on the next upkeep, but Mishra's Bauble looks at the top of any player's library (setting up Brainstorm and providing information) while Urza's Bauble looks at a player's hand (less useful when you already have Thoughtseize). Mishra's Bauble also counts as an artifact for delirium, Saga, and artifact-matters strategies, making it a staple while Urza's Bauble is forgotten.

Yes, Boseiju, Who Endures is Legacy-playable as a utility land. Its channel ability destroys a target artifact, enchantment, or nonbasic land for 1G at instant speed with the uncounterable channel keyword. As a land, it has almost zero opportunity cost in green decks. It answers Urza's Saga, Chalice of the Void, Blood Moon, and other problem permanents while being a Forest when you don't need it. It's a staple 1-2 of in green sideboards and maindecks.

Shardless Agent is barely Legacy-playable in the current format. A 2/2 for 1GU that cascades into a spell with mana value 2 or less was once a powerhouse in BUG Cascade, but the format has sped up considerably. Cascading into Hymn to Tourach, Brainstorm, or Baleful Strix was strong, but 3 mana for a 2/2 with random value is too inconsistent now. It occasionally appears in fringe builds but is no longer a format player.

Yes, Trinisphere is Legacy-playable in prison and Stompy strategies. Making all spells cost at least 3 mana is devastating against cantrip-heavy blue decks and Storm combo. Deployed off Ancient Tomb or City of Traitors on turn 1, it can lock opponents out entirely. It's a staple in Moon Stompy and other prison builds, though it requires building around since it affects your own spells too.

Yes, Teferi, Time Raveler is Legacy-playable in control and combo strategies. For 1WU, his static ability prevents opponents from casting spells at instant speed, shutting down Force of Will, Brainstorm, and all countermagic. His -3 bounces a permanent and draws a card, providing immediate value. He's excellent in Show and Tell decks to prevent interaction on your combo turn and in control as a disruption piece.

instruction: How would a 2-mana instant that exiles all cards in all graveyards and draws a card affect Legacy?
output: This would be a powerful sideboard staple and possibly maindeckable in some metagames. Compare to Rest in Peace (2 mana, permanent but no cantrip) and Surgical Extraction (free but only one card). A cantripping instant-speed graveyard wipe for 2 mana is excellent against Reanimator, Dredge, Murktide, and Uro. The card replacement makes it low-cost to include. It would likely become the default graveyard hate option for blue-based decks.

instruction: How would a 1-mana white creature with 'creatures your opponents control enter tapped' affect Legacy?
output: This would be strong in Legacy, especially in D&T. Forcing all opposing creatures to enter tapped shuts down flash creatures (Bowmasters, Endurance), prevents surprise blockers, and slows down aggro. At 1 mana it's efficiently costed, though as a creature it dies to removal. Compare to Blind Obedience (2 mana enchantment) which is harder to remove. This would be a staple in white creature decks and a strong metagame choice when creature-heavy strategies dominate.

instruction: How would a 3-mana artifact that says 'players can't search their libraries' affect Legacy?
output: A 3-mana Stranglehold on an artifact would be very impactful in Legacy. It shuts down fetchlands (the backbone of every mana base), tutors like Entomb and Green Sun's Zenith, and Stoneforge Mystic's ETB. However, at 3 mana it's a turn slower than ideal, and it doesn't affect the board immediately. It would be a powerful Stompy piece if deployed off fast mana but too slow for fair decks. Compare to Aven Mindcensor which does this on a flash body.

instruction: How would a 1-mana blue creature with flash and 'when this enters, look at the top 3 cards of your library, put one in hand and the rest on the bottom' affect Legacy?
output: A 1-mana flash creature that Impulses on ETB would be extremely strong in Legacy. Instant-speed card selection on a body is premium, and at 1 mana it slots into any blue deck. It provides a body for attacking, blocking Bowmasters tokens, and carrying equipment, while also replacing itself with selection. Compare to Snapcaster Mage (2 mana, flashback instead of selection). This would likely be a format staple and possibly too strong depending on its stats.

instruction: How would a land that enters tapped and has 'tap: add one mana of any color; tap, sacrifice: draw a card' affect Legacy?
output: An ETB-tapped land with any-color mana and a sacrifice-to-draw option would be niche at best. Legacy's mana demands are too tight for tapped lands; losing a turn of tempo is often fatal against Delver or combo. Compare to Horizon lands (Horizon Canopy, etc.) which enter untapped and sacrifice to draw. The any-color fixing doesn't compensate for entering tapped. It might see fringe play in slow control decks but wouldn't be a format staple.

instruction: Compare Careful Study vs Faithless Looting in Legacy.
output: Both enable graveyard strategies at 1 mana. Careful Study (U sorcery) draws 2, discards 2 once. Faithless Looting (R sorcery) draws 2, discards 2, with flashback for 2R. Looting is generally better because the flashback provides a second use, doubling its value for graveyard strategies. However, Careful Study is blue, which matters for Force of Will pitch count. Looting sees more play in Painter and red graveyard decks, while Study appears in blue-based Reanimator.

instruction: Compare Leyline of the Void vs Surgical Extraction as graveyard hate in Legacy.
output: Both fight graveyard decks but with different approaches. Leyline of the Void (free in opening hand, or 2BB) exiles everything that would enter an opponent's graveyard, providing blanket prevention. Surgical Extraction (free via Phyrexian mana) exiles all copies of one specific card. Leyline is better against Dredge and broadly graveyard-dependent decks. Surgical is better as surgical disruption against specific combo pieces and provides hand information. Many sideboards mix both approaches.

Yes, Spell Pierce is Legacy-playable as a cheap and flexible counterspell. For U, it counters a noncreature spell unless its controller pays 2, which is excellent in the early game against cantrips, removal, and combo pieces. It falls off in the late game when opponents can afford the 2 mana tax, but in tempo-oriented decks, it buys critical time. It sees play in Delver sideboards and occasionally maindecks depending on the metagame.

Satoru, the Infiltrator has seen some Legacy experimentation. For UB, a 2/3 menace that draws a card whenever a creature you didn't cast enters the battlefield is interesting with Aether Vial, token generation, and Reanimator effects. The body is reasonably sized for 2 mana, and the card draw can be significant in the right shell. It's fringe playable in specific builds but hasn't established itself as a format staple.

instruction: Compare Unholy Heat vs Lightning Bolt in Legacy.
output: Both cost R for instant-speed damage. Lightning Bolt always deals 3, while Unholy Heat deals 2 normally but 6 with delirium. In delirium-focused decks with DRC, Mishra's Bauble, and fetchlands, Unholy Heat is often better because 6 damage kills Murktide Regent, Uro, and other large creatures that survive Bolt. Without reliable delirium, Bolt is more consistent. Many Izzet Delver lists split between both or lean toward Unholy Heat for the higher ceiling.

Yes, Urza's Saga is one of the most impactful cards in Legacy. As a land that creates growing construct tokens and tutors for 0-1 mana artifacts, it provides both threats and utility. It finds Retrofitter Foundry, Shadowspear, Pithing Needle, and other silver bullets. The construct tokens can be 6/6 or larger in artifact-heavy decks. It's a multi-archetype staple appearing in 8-Cast, D&T, Painter, and various other strategies.

instruction: How would a 2-mana creature with 'whenever you cast a noncreature spell, create a 1/1 token' affect Legacy?
output: This would be very strong in Legacy's cantrip-heavy environment. Casting Brainstorm, Ponder, and other cheap spells would generate an army of tokens rapidly. Compare to Young Pyromancer (2 mana, same effect) which has historically been a solid Legacy card. If this creature had better stats or an additional ability beyond Young Pyromancer's baseline, it could be a format staple. At Young Pyromancer's rate (2/1), it's already proven to be playable.

instruction: Compare Life from the Loam vs Crucible of Worlds in Legacy.
output: Both recur lands from the graveyard but through different mechanisms. Life from the Loam (1G sorcery with dredge 3) returns up to 3 lands to hand and has dredge for repeatable self-mill. Crucible of Worlds (3 mana artifact) lets you play lands from the graveyard continuously. Loam is better in Lands because dredge fuels graveyard synergies and it recovers from Wasteland wars. Crucible is more straightforward but costs more and lacks dredge synergy. Loam is the Legacy staple; Crucible is the backup.

Yes, Ledger Shredder is Legacy-playable in blue tempo and midrange strategies. For 1U, a 1/3 flyer that connives whenever any player casts their second spell in a turn is excellent in a format where multiple spells per turn are the norm. It grows quickly, filters your draws, and fills the graveyard for delve. It competes with Murktide and DRC for deck slots but offers a different angle as a scaling threat that rewards the cantrip playstyle.

instruction: How would a 0-mana instant that says 'counter target spell that targets you or a permanent you control' affect Legacy?
output: A free defensive counterspell would be very strong in Legacy. It protects your threats from Swords to Plowshares, your planeswalkers from targeted effects, and you from targeted discard like Thoughtseize. Compare to Force of Will which requires pitching a card. A truly free counter, even narrow, is dangerous. It would make resolved threats much harder to remove and would be a 4-of in almost every deck. It might be too strong depending on exact scope.

instruction: Compare Stoneforge Mystic vs Puresteel Paladin for equipment strategies in Legacy.
output: Stoneforge Mystic is far superior in Legacy. For 1W, she tutors any equipment and cheats it into play at instant speed for 1W, bypassing the mana cost of expensive equipment like Kaldra Compleat. Puresteel Paladin (WW) draws cards when equipment enters and enables free equip costs with metalcraft, but requires already having equipment in play and hitting metalcraft. Stoneforge is a self-contained package; Puresteel requires too much support and is not Legacy-playable.

Yes, Blood Moon is a Legacy staple in red prison strategies. For 2R, turning all nonbasic lands into Mountains is devastating in a format built on dual lands, fetchlands, and utility lands. Many Legacy decks run few or no basic lands, so a resolved Blood Moon can effectively lock opponents out of casting spells. It's a core piece of Moon Stompy and a strong sideboard option for any red deck.

instruction: How would a 2-mana enchantment that says 'at the beginning of your upkeep, surveil 2' affect Legacy?
output: A 2-mana repeatable surveil 2 would be solid but not exceptional in Legacy. Compare to Sylvan Library (1G, draws 2 extra) which is much more powerful. Surveil 2 provides card selection and graveyard filling, which benefits delirium, delve, and reanimator strategies. However, it doesn't actually draw cards, making it weaker than most Legacy card advantage options. It would see niche play in graveyard-focused decks but wouldn't be a format staple.

Yes, Invasion of Gobakhan sees Legacy play in D&T and white midrange strategies. For 1W, it functions as a Tidehollow Sculler-style effect that exiles a nonland card from the opponent's hand, with the battle backside providing additional value if flipped. The ETB hand disruption on a white permanent that can be flickered with Flickerwisp or Yorion makes it a useful tool in white creature decks.

instruction: Compare Chalice of the Void vs Trinisphere as lock pieces in Legacy.
output: Both restrict spell casting but in different ways. Chalice on X counters all spells with mana value X, and on 1 it shuts down Brainstorm, Ponder, Swords, and most cantrips. Trinisphere makes all spells cost at least 3, which is broader but doesn't outright counter anything. Chalice is more devastating on specific values (1 is backbreaking) but can be played around. Trinisphere is more universally taxing but lets opponents still cast spells if they have mana. Most Stompy decks play Chalice; some run both.

Yes, it is the best creature removal spell ever printed. For just W at instant speed, it exiles a creature permanently. Exile is better than destroy (no graveyard recursion, handles indestructible). The life gain for the opponent is a real cost against combo where every point matters, but against creature decks it is negligible. Swords is in 21% of Legacy decks and is an automatic 4-of in any white deck.

instruction: Is Chalice of the Void Legacy-playable?
output: Yes, it is one of the most format-defining cards. On turn 1 from Ancient Tomb, Chalice on 1 counters Brainstorm, Ponder, Swords to Plowshares, Lightning Bolt, Thoughtseize, Daze, and most of Legacy's best spells. It is the backbone of every prison/stompy strategy (Eldrazi, Red Stompy, Affinity). At 33% of decks running Ancient Tomb and many running Chalice, it is a format pillar.

instruction: Is Uro, Titan of Nature's Wrath Legacy-playable?
output: Yes, though less dominant than it once was. Uro provides 3 life, draws a card, and ramps for 1UG on cast, then escapes from the graveyard as a 6/6 for the same effects repeatedly. In grindy control mirrors, Uro is nearly unbeatable card advantage. It sees play in Bant and 4-color control builds. The 3-mana initial cost and graveyard dependence are real drawbacks in a format with Surgical Extraction and Leyline of the Void.

Yes, it is the best targeted hand disruption spell. For B and 2 life, you see the opponent's entire hand and take any nonland card. It is in 43% of Legacy decks. Thoughtseize is proactive disruption that works against every strategy: take combo pieces, take removal, take countermagic. The 2 life cost is minimal in a format where games are often decided by card quality rather than life totals. Automatic 4-of in any black tempo or combo deck.

Yes, in the right shell. Mentor creates a 1/1 Monk token with prowess for every noncreature spell you cast. In a cantrip-heavy deck, Mentor can create 3-5 tokens in a single turn, each of which gets prowess bonuses. It sees play in UW Control and Jeskai builds as a win condition that ends games in 2-3 turns. The 3-mana cost is high for Legacy, but the explosive potential justifies it.

Marginally. Snapcaster Mage was excellent in Legacy for years, but the format has moved away from it. At 1U for a 2/1 flash that gives a spell flashback, the rate is good. However, Orcish Bowmasters kills it on sight while generating value, and the 2-mana cost competes with more impactful plays. It still sees fringe play in control decks but is no longer a staple. It was better when the format was slower.

Only in Burn. Goblin Guide is a 2/2 haste for R, which is an aggressive rate, but the card reveal drawback is significant and the body is fragile. In Burn, you do not care about card advantage - you want to deal maximum damage before the opponent stabilizes. Goblin Guide represents 4-6 damage over 2-3 turns. Outside of Burn, it is too small and provides too much information to the opponent.

instruction: Is Jace, the Mind Sculptor Legacy-playable?
output: Yes, though less dominant than in previous eras. Jace costs 2UU and provides: +2 to control opponent's draws, 0 to Brainstorm every turn, -1 to bounce a creature, and -12 to win the game. In control decks, Jace is one of the best win conditions because it provides card advantage every turn while threatening an ultimate. At 4 mana, it is too slow for tempo but perfect for control. It competes with faster card advantage like Up the Beanstalk.

Barely, and mostly no. Counterspell costs UU to counter any spell, which seems great, but Legacy has Force of Will (free), Daze (free), Force of Negation (free on opponent's turn), and Flusterstorm (U for storm). Paying 2 mana to counter a spell is too slow when your opponent's spells cost 0-1 mana. The double-blue requirement is also restrictive. Counterspell sees occasional play in UW Control but has been largely replaced by more efficient alternatives.

Barely, and declining. Tarmogoyf was Legacy's premier threat for a decade, but Barrowgoyf has mostly replaced it. Both grow based on card types in graveyards, but Barrowgoyf does not count the opponent's graveyard (which matters with Leyline of the Void) and has a more relevant creature type. More importantly, Murktide Regent is simply better: it flies, dodges Bolt, and is often larger. Goyf at 1G for a 4/5 is still powerful, but the format has outgrown it.

No. Mana Leak costs 1U and counters unless the opponent pays 3. In Legacy, this is worse than Daze (free), Force of Will (free), Spell Pierce (U, pay 2), and Flusterstorm (U, uncounterable vs instants/sorceries). By turn 3-4, opponents always have 3 mana to pay. Mana Leak is a Modern card that does not meet Legacy's efficiency standards. Every slot spent on Mana Leak would be better spent on a free or 1-mana counter.

No. Doom Blade costs 1B and destroys a nonblack creature. In Legacy, this fails on multiple levels: it costs 2 mana (too expensive for removal), it does not exile (important against recursion), it misses black creatures (Orcish Bowmasters, the most played creature, is black), and it destroys rather than exiling (Swords to Plowshares does both for W). Fatal Push at B is strictly better in almost every case. Snuff Out at 4 life is free. Legacy demands extreme mana efficiency from removal.

No, or very rarely. Opt costs U to scry 1 then draw 1, seeing only 2 cards. Brainstorm (U, draw 3 put back 2 with shuffle) and Ponder (U, look at 3 with shuffle option) both see more cards for the same cost. Even Preordain (U, scry 2 draw 1) is better since it sees 3 cards. The only case for Opt is if you already play 4 Brainstorm + 4 Ponder and need a 9th cantrip, but even then Consider or Preordain are usually preferred.

It WAS Legacy-playable and was in fact banned for being too good. Ragavan was a 1-mana 2/1 with dash that created a Treasure token and exiled the top card of the opponent's library (castable) on combat damage. A turn-1 Ragavan that connected generated mana AND card advantage, snowballing games immediately. It was in every UR/URx deck at high percentages. It was banned because a 1-drop should not generate that much advantage with no deckbuilding cost.

No. Lightning Helix costs RW for 3 damage and 3 life. In Legacy, Lightning Bolt costs R for 3 damage. The extra W and 3 life do not justify an additional color requirement. Legacy removal needs to be as mana-efficient as possible. Helix would be fine in Standard but fails the Legacy test: is it better than the cheapest available alternative? Against Bolt, it is not. The life gain is negligible in a format where games are decided by card quality.

Yes, in artifact-heavy decks. Thoughtcast has affinity for artifacts, often costing U to draw 2 cards. In 8-Cast, Affinity Stompy, and Blue Artifacts decks, Thoughtcast is a 1-mana draw-2, which is absurdly efficient. It is unplayable outside dedicated artifact decks since without 4+ artifacts in play, it costs too much. In the right shell, it is one of the best card advantage spells in Legacy.

instruction: Toxic Deluge vs Terminus: which board wipe is better?
output: Depends on the deck. Terminus (miracle W, regular 4WW) puts all creatures on the bottom of libraries - dodges indestructible and prevents graveyard triggers. Best in decks with cantrips that can set up the miracle. Toxic Deluge (1BB, pay X life for -X/-X) costs life but kills at instant-speed priority, bypasses indestructible, and scales precisely. Best in black decks that need a flexible sweeper. Terminus is better against True-Name Nemesis (protection does not stop non-targeting). Deluge is better against haste creatures and when you need a sweeper NOW.

instruction: Surgical Extraction vs Extirpate: what is the difference?
output: Surgical Extraction costs 2 life (Phyrexian mana) to exile all copies of a card from opponent's hand, graveyard, and library. Extirpate costs B and has split second (cannot be responded to except by mana abilities). In Legacy, Surgical is far more played because: 1) It costs 0 mana (pay life), allowing use while tapped out. 2) Split second from Extirpate is rarely relevant since the effect is instant-speed anyway. 3) Not needing B lets non-black decks play Surgical. Extirpate's one advantage: it stops Brainstorm in response (split second prevents casting).

instruction: Force of Vigor vs Nature's Claim: which artifact/enchantment removal is better?
output: Force of Vigor costs 2GG or pitches a green card for free to destroy up to two artifacts and/or enchantments. Nature's Claim costs G to destroy one artifact or enchantment but gains the opponent 4 life. Force of Vigor is better in most Legacy contexts: it is free (critical against Chalice on 1 which prevents casting Claim), it destroys TWO targets (crucial against decks with multiple problematic artifacts), and Legacy games rarely care about 4 life. Claim is better when mana is plentiful and you only need to hit one target, or when the 4 life matters against Burn.

instruction: Leyline of the Void vs Rest in Peace: which graveyard hate is better?
output: Both exile cards that would go to the graveyard. Leyline starts in play for free if in your opening hand. Rest in Peace costs 1W and also exiles all cards currently in graveyards when it enters. Leyline is better in non-white decks (any deck can play it), against turn-1 combos (it is already in play), and when you use your own graveyard (it only affects opponents). Rest in Peace is better in white decks (castable if not in opener), when you need to clear existing graveyards, and combined with Helm of Obedience (combo). Most Legacy decks prefer Leyline due to the free start.

instruction: Endurance vs Leyline of the Void: which graveyard hate is better?
output: Different purposes. Leyline is a hard lock: cards never reach the graveyard at all. It completely shuts down Dredge, Reanimator, and Oops. Endurance is a one-shot effect: flash in, shuffle a graveyard into library. It delays graveyard strategies one turn but does not prevent them from rebuilding. Endurance's advantages: it is a 3/4 body with reach (relevant blocker), it works with Green Sun's Zenith, and it does not hurt your own graveyard. Leyline wins the graveyard matchup harder; Endurance is more flexible overall.

instruction: Dark Confidant vs Sylvan Library: which card advantage engine is better?
output: In the current format, Sylvan Library is significantly better. Dark Confidant is a 2/1 for 1B that draws an extra card per turn at the cost of life equal to its mana value. Orcish Bowmasters kills Confidant immediately while generating value, making it a liability. Sylvan Library costs 1G as an enchantment (harder to remove), lets you look at 3 cards per draw step, and choose to keep extras for 4 life each. Library provides better card selection without being vulnerable to creature removal.

instruction: Delver of Secrets vs Dragon's Rage Channeler: which 1-drop threat is better?
output: In the current format, Delver of Secrets is generally better. Delver flips into a 3/2 flyer which dodges ground blockers and attacks through board stalls. Dragon's Rage Channeler is a 1/1 that surveys (look at top, may put in graveyard) and becomes a 3/3 with flying at delirium (4 card types in graveyard). DRC was dominant alongside Ragavan, but post-Ragavan ban and with Orcish Bowmasters punishing 1-toughness creatures, DRC's 1/1 body is a liability. Delver's 0/1 back side survives Bowmasters' 1-damage ping before flipping.

instruction: Wasteland vs Strip Mine: why is Strip Mine banned?
output: Strip Mine destroys any land (including basics) with no mana cost beyond tapping and sacrificing itself. Wasteland only destroys nonbasic lands. This seemingly small difference is enormous: Strip Mine lets you keep an opponent permanently off mana by recurring it (with Life from the Loam or Crucible of Worlds), targeting their basics after they fetch them. Wasteland at least lets opponents fetch basic lands to play around it. Strip Mine would make Lands and any deck with recursion able to achieve a complete mana lock, which is considered too oppressive for Legacy.

instruction: Show and Tell vs Natural Order: which cheat effect is better?
output: Different strengths. Show and Tell (2U) puts any permanent from hand into play - your Emrakul, Omniscience, or Sneak Attack. Weakness: opponent also puts something in play. Natural Order (2GG) sacrifices a green creature to tutor any green creature directly into play. Weakness: needs a creature to sacrifice and only finds green creatures. Show and Tell is better for putting non-creature/non-green permanents into play and does not require a board. Natural Order is better when you want a specific creature (Craterhoof Behemoth) and already have a sacrifice-worthy creature (any elf).

instruction: Brazen Borrower vs Vendilion Clique: which 3-mana flash flyer is better?
output: Context-dependent. Brazen Borrower bounces any nonland permanent as an adventure (Petty Theft, 1U instant), then can be cast as a 3/1 flash flyer for 1UU. Vendilion Clique is a 3/1 flash flyer for 1UU that looks at an opponent's hand and can exchange one card for a random draw. Borrower is better for board interaction (bouncing resolved threats) and against decks with few cards in hand. Clique is better for information and surgical disruption against combo (strip a key piece). In Dimir Tempo, Clique's information synergizes better with Thoughtseize.

Yes, it is one of the most important lands in Legacy. Ancient Tomb taps for 2 colorless mana at the cost of 2 life. It enables turn-1 plays that normally cost 2 mana: Chalice of the Void on 1, Painter's Servant, and many others. It is in 33% of Legacy decks. The 2 life per use adds up, but prison and combo decks plan to end the game before life totals matter. Ancient Tomb is the enabler for every Stompy, Eldrazi, and artifact strategy.

Marginally. Preordain is the third-best cantrip in Legacy after Brainstorm and Ponder, and most decks only need 8 cantrips (4+4). It sees play as cantrips 9-10 in combo decks that need maximum card selection (some Storm builds, some Doomsday lists) and in decks without fetchlands where Ponder's shuffle is less valuable. It costs U to scry 2 then draw 1. The scry-before-draw sequencing is decent but seeing only 3 cards vs Ponder's 4 (with shuffle) is a real downside.

Yes, it is one of the most impactful Modern Horizons 2 additions to Legacy. Urza's Saga is in 22% of decks. As a land, it costs zero deck slots (replaces a land). Chapter I and II create Construct tokens that grow with artifact count. Chapter III tutors for any 0-1 mana artifact (Pithing Needle, Shadowspear, Aether Spellbomb, etc.). It provides both a threat and a tutor on a land. The downside is vulnerability to Wasteland and Blood Moon, and it self-destructs after chapter III.

Yes. Karakas is a legendary land that taps for W and has an ability to return target legendary creature to its owner's hand. It is in 21% of Legacy decks. It bounces opposing Emrakul, Atraxa, Thalia, and any other legend for free. It protects your own legends from removal (return Thalia to hand in response to Swords). It costs no mana and no card to activate. The only cost is that it is a nonbasic land (vulnerable to Wasteland) and legendary (second copy is dead). Every white deck should consider Karakas.

Yes, though less dominant than it once was. True-Name Nemesis is a 3/1 for 1UU with protection from the chosen player. It cannot be targeted, damaged, blocked, or enchanted by that player. This makes it essentially unremovable through normal means. Answers: Toxic Deluge, Terminus, sacrifice effects, Council's Judgment. At 3 mana it is slow for Legacy, but the unkillable clock is valuable in grindy matchups. It has declined because Bowmasters and faster threats have made 3-mana creatures less appealing.

Entomb WAS extremely Legacy-playable but was banned in November 2025. For B at instant speed, it put any card from your library into your graveyard. This enabled turn-1 kills with Reanimate: turn 1 Entomb for Griselbrand, Reanimate it, draw 14 cards, win. The card was too efficient as a combo enabler with zero deckbuilding cost. Post-ban, Reanimator still exists but uses slower discard outlets like Faithful Mending.

Yes. Up the Beanstalk is a 2-mana enchantment that draws a card on ETB and draws again whenever you cast a spell with mana value 5 or greater. In Legacy, Force of Will (MV 5), Solitude (MV 5), Fury (MV 5), and Subtlety (MV 5) all trigger it even when cast for free alternative costs. This turns pitch spells into cantrips. Beanstalk Control at 2.3% meta share is built entirely around this interaction.

In specific shells, yes. Walking Ballista costs XX and enters with X +1/+1 counters, with an ability to remove a counter to deal 1 damage to any target. At face value, this is too slow and expensive for Legacy. But as a combo kill condition, it is perfect: with infinite mana from Food Chain, Auriok Salvagers + LED, or Mystic Forge loops, Ballista kills by entering with infinite counters. It is also tutorable by Karn, the Great Creator from the sideboard. As a standalone card, it is not Legacy-playable; as a combo piece, it is excellent.

instruction: Is Narset, Parter of Veils Legacy-playable?
output: Situationally yes.
