Narset costs 1UU, has a static ability that prevents opponents from drawing more than one card per turn (shuts off Brainstorm, Ponder, Sylvan Library, Glimpse of Nature), and a -2 to look at top 4 and take a noncreature, nonland card. Against cantrip-heavy decks, Narset is devastating. Against combo or non-blue decks, the passive is less relevant. She sees sideboard play in UW/UWR control as a meta-dependent card. The 3 mana cost is her biggest limitation.

It WAS extremely Legacy-playable and was banned in March 2023. For UR, you looked at the top 3 cards, put one in hand, one in exile (playable this turn), and one on the bottom. This was effectively draw-2 for 2 mana with card selection. It was in every UR and URx deck and gave Delver too-efficient card advantage. After the ban, Delver decks lost their best card advantage spell and had to adapt with less efficient options.

Yes, Daze is one of the defining cards of Legacy. For its alternative cost of returning an Island to your hand, you counter a spell unless the opponent pays 1. Daze is in 27% of Legacy decks. It is at its best on the play in the first 2-3 turns when opponents tap out. Combined with Wasteland (destroy the land they would use to pay), Daze becomes a hard counter. It is worse in long games when opponents have surplus mana. Best in: Delver, tempo. Worst in: control, combo.

instruction: Is Orcish Bowmasters the best creature in Legacy?
output: It has a strong case. Bowmasters is in 29% of decks - the most played creature in Legacy. It has flash (instant-speed deployment), triggers on every opponent card draw beyond the first (punishing cantrips), creates a growing Orc Army token, and costs just 1B. It reshuffled the entire metagame when printed: Elves and small-creature strategies collapsed, cantrip timing became a strategic decision, and every black deck gained a premium threat. The only argument against it being the best: Murktide Regent closes games faster and dodges Bolt, while Bowmasters is more of a value engine.

Yes, in the right shells. Hymn to Tourach costs BB and forces the opponent to discard 2 cards at random. The random discard is both its strength (can hit any card including lands) and weakness (might hit irrelevant cards). It is devastating on turn 1 with Dark Ritual or turn 2 normally, stripping 2 cards when hands are small. It sees play in Pox, Mono Black, and some BW builds. The double-black cost limits it to heavily black decks. Hymn has declined because Thoughtseize's surgical precision is often better than random 2-for-1.

Yes, in artifact-heavy decks. Mox Opal costs 0 and taps for any color with metalcraft (3+ artifacts). In 8-Cast, Affinity, and artifact combo decks, metalcraft is trivially achieved on turn 1 (artifact lands + 0-cost artifacts). A free mana source in these decks is absurdly powerful. Outside dedicated artifact decks, metalcraft is too unreliable. Mox Opal is in about 14% of Legacy decks, concentrated entirely in artifact strategies. It is one of the best fast mana sources in the format.

Grief WAS Legacy-playable and was banned in 2024. For 2BB or by pitching a black card, Grief was a 3/2 menace with an ETB that Thoughtseized the opponent. The broken interaction was Grief + Ephemerate: turn 1, pitch Grief for free, Thoughtseize them, Ephemerate Grief (blink it, dodging the evoke sacrifice), Thoughtseize them again. On your opponent's upkeep, Ephemerate rebounds and Thoughtseizes a third time. This left the opponent with 4 fewer cards by turn 2 while you had a 3/2. It was banned for creating unacceptable non-games.

Yes, it is the backbone of Death and Taxes and several other creature-based strategies. Aether Vial costs 1 and puts creatures into play at instant speed without paying mana, bypassing counterspells (Daze, Force of Will, Chalice). It gains a charge counter each upkeep, matching your creature curve. At 2 counters it puts in Thalia, Stoneforge, Phyrexian Revoker. At 3 it puts in Flickerwisp, Recruiter, Skyclave Apparition. The instant-speed deployment creates combat tricks and EOT value that no other card enables.

Yes, Blood Moon is a format-defining prison piece. For 2R, all nonbasic lands become Mountains. In a format where most decks run 15+ nonbasic lands, Blood Moon effectively turns off the opponent's mana base. It is best in mono-red decks (Red Stompy, Painter) that only need Mountains anyway. It is in about 10-15% of Legacy decks mainboard or sideboard. The card single-handedly wins games against greedy multi-color decks that did not fetch basics early enough.

Yes, in Lands and some other land-focused strategies. Crop Rotation is a 1-mana green instant that sacrifices a land to tutor any land card directly into play. This is enormously powerful in Legacy because lands are powerful: Dark Depths (20/20 combo), Thespian's Stage (copy combo lands), Bojuka Bog (graveyard hate), Karakas (bounce legends), Maze of Ith (fog attackers), Wasteland (destroy lands), Tabernacle (kill creatures). Crop Rotation is an instant-speed tutor for any of these effects at just 1 mana.

Barely, and in very specific shells. Collected Company costs 3G and looks at the top 6 cards, putting up to 2 creatures with mana value 3 or less onto the battlefield. At 4 mana, it competes with Natural Order (which gets ANY green creature including Craterhoof). CoCo is better when you want creature density and instant-speed deployment, worse when you want a specific finisher. It sees occasional play in Maverick and GW creature decks but is not a Legacy staple. The 4-mana cost and randomness work against it in a format that demands precision.

Yes. The One Ring costs 4 mana and provides: indestructible and protection from everything until your next turn (survives a full turn cycle safely), and an activated ability to draw cards (tap, put a burden counter, draw cards equal to burden counters). The card advantage is absurd - drawing 1, then 2, then 3 cards per turn. The life loss from burden counters eventually kills you, but prison decks lock the opponent before that matters. It is in about 13% of Legacy decks, primarily in Red Stompy, Mystic Forge, and Eldrazi.

Situationally yes. Stifle costs U and counters any activated or triggered ability. It counters fetchland activations (1-mana Stone Rain), Storm triggers (stops all copies), cascade triggers, and ETB effects. It was a staple in RUG Delver alongside Wasteland for mana denial. In the current meta, Stifle is less common because Dimir Tempo prefers Thoughtseize for proactive disruption. But in Stiflenought (Stifle + Phyrexian Dreadnought), it is essential. Meta-dependent card that shines when opponents rely on activated/triggered abilities.

Yes, specifically in Lands. Punishing Fire costs 1R for 2 damage to any target. With Grove of the Burnwillows (which gains the opponent 1 life when tapped for mana), you can return Punishing Fire from the graveyard to your hand whenever an opponent gains life. This creates an infinite loop: tap Grove (opponent gains 1 life), return Fire, cast Fire (deal 2 damage). This grinds out any creature-based strategy over time. The combo sees play almost exclusively in Lands where Grove is a natural mana source.

Yes, it is one of the most played sideboard cards at 25% of decks. Surgical costs 0 mana (2 life via Phyrexian mana) and exiles all copies of a named card from opponent's hand, graveyard, and library. Key targets: Thassa's Oracle (kills Doomsday and Oops), Life from the Loam (cripples Lands), Reanimate targets (stops Reanimator), Show and Tell (leaves them with creatures they cannot cheat). The 0 mana cost means you can use it while tapped out. The hand and library exile provides information and removes future threats.

instruction: Is Delver of Secrets still Legacy-playable in 2026?
output: Yes, but less than its peak. Delver of Secrets is technically a 1-mana creature that flips into a 3/2 flyer - an excellent rate. In Izzet Delver (4.3% meta), it remains the namesake threat. However, Dimir Tempo (14.6%) has largely moved away from Delver in favor of Barrowgoyf and Tamiyo, which provide more consistent value without requiring the coin-flip of Delver's upkeep trigger. Delver is still Legacy-playable but is no longer the default 1-drop in the best blue tempo deck.

Yes, as both maindeck and sideboard card. Endurance is a 3/4 reach for 1GG or pitch a green card for free. The ETB shuffles a target player's graveyard into their library. This serves as: instant-speed graveyard hate (disrupts Reanimator, Dredge, Doomsday piles), a flash blocker (3/4 reach blocks Murktide Regent for a turn), a green pitch for Force of Vigor, and a Green Sun's Zenith target. At 8% of decks, it is the premier green sideboard card and sometimes makes the main in BUG and Bant builds.

Yes, as a powerful sideboard card. For G, Veil of Summer makes you and your spells uncounterable by blue or black spells, prevents you from being targeted by blue or black spells, and draws a card if an opponent has cast a blue or black spell this turn. In Legacy, where blue (Force of Will, Daze) and black (Thoughtseize, Fatal Push) dominate interaction, Veil shuts down an entire turn of disruption for 1 mana while replacing itself. It is the green equivalent of Pyroblast but broader.

Only in Burn. Swiftspear is a 1/2 haste with prowess for R. In Burn, it attacks for 1 immediately and grows with each burn spell cast (prowess triggers). A typical Burn turn: Swiftspear, Bolt, Bolt = Swiftspear attacks for 3 (base 1 + 2 prowess) plus 6 from Bolts = 9 damage. Outside of Burn, the 1/2 body is too fragile for Legacy (dies to Bowmasters' ping) and lacks the card advantage or utility that Legacy creatures need.

Yes, it is one of the best card advantage engines in Legacy. For 1G, Sylvan Library lets you draw 2 extra cards during your draw step, then choose to put any back (or keep for 4 life each). This is effectively Ancestral Recall every turn with a life cost. In green midrange and control decks, Library dominates long games by providing unmatched card selection. It is harder to remove than creatures (enchantment, not vulnerable to Bowmasters or Bolt) and does not require mana to activate each turn. It is in about 5-8% of Legacy decks, primarily in green-based fair strategies.

Yes, in combo decks. Pact of Negation costs 0 mana to counter a spell, with the upkeep cost of paying 3UU or losing the game. In combo decks that win on the turn they cast Pact, the upkeep cost never comes due. Oops All Spells uses it to protect the combo turn. Storm uses it as backup protection. Show and Tell uses it to force through the combo. It is NOT playable in fair decks because the upkeep cost of 5 mana is untenable in a deck that plans to survive to next turn. Pact is strictly a same-turn-win protection spell.

Yes, in prison and combo decks. Chrome Mox costs 0 and taps for any color of the imprinted card's colors. The imprint cost (exile a nonland, nonartifact card from hand) is significant card disadvantage, but the free mana enables turn-1 lock pieces (Blood Moon, Chalice, Trinisphere) that win the game outright. It is in Red Stompy, some Painter builds, and occasional combo lists. Not playable in fair decks where card advantage matters more than speed. Chrome Mox at $50-70 is a budget alternative to Mox Diamond ($400-500).

Yes, it is the backbone of green toolbox strategies. GSZ costs XG and searches your library for a green creature with mana value X or less and puts it directly onto the battlefield. For X=0, it finds Dryad Arbor (free mana dork). For X=2, it finds Collector Ouphe or Gaddock Teeg. For X=3, it finds Knight of the Reliquary or Endurance. For X=5+, it finds Craterhoof Behemoth. The toolbox flexibility in one card is unmatched. GSZ then shuffles into your library, not the graveyard, so you can draw it again. It is in Elves, Maverick, Cradle Control, and Nic Fit.

instruction: Is Thoughtseize or Inquisition of Kozilek better in Legacy?
output: Thoughtseize is significantly better. Inquisition of Kozilek hits nonland cards with MV 3 or less, which misses many critical Legacy targets: Force of Will (MV 5), Murktide Regent (MV 7), Show and Tell (MV 3... actually IoK does hit MV 3 or less, so Show and Tell IS hit). However, IoK misses Emrakul, Omniscience, Sneak Attack (MV 4), Force of Will, Force of Negation (MV 3, IS hit). The life cost of Thoughtseize (2 life) is worth the ability to hit ANY nonland card. Legacy staples like Force of Will and expensive combo pieces are too important to miss.

instruction: Is Lightning Bolt or Chain Lightning better in Legacy Burn?
output: Lightning Bolt is better overall because it is an instant (can respond to threats, deal damage at end of turn). Chain Lightning is a sorcery that deals 3 damage for R but the opponent can copy it for RR (rarely relevant in Legacy since they would rather not take 3). Burn plays 4 of BOTH because it needs maximum damage density. If forced to choose, Bolt wins: instant speed matters against creatures (kill Bowmasters in response to its trigger) and for end-of-turn damage (represent Bolt to force opponent into suboptimal plays). Chain Lightning is the 5th-8th copy of Bolt, which is still excellent.

instruction: Is Swords to Plowshares or Path to Exile better in Legacy?
output: Swords to Plowshares is dramatically better. Both cost W and exile a creature, but Swords gives the opponent life while Path gives them a land. In Legacy, life is nearly irrelevant (games are decided by board state and card quality, not life totals). But giving an opponent a land is devastating: it counteracts your Wasteland strategy, helps them cast spells through Daze, and ramps them toward their combo turn. Path to Exile is barely playable in Legacy specifically because the land ramp downside is too severe. Swords is in 21% of Legacy decks; Path is in less than 1%.

Marginally. Kolaghan's Command costs 1BR and has 4 modes (choose 2): deal 2 damage to any target, return a creature from graveyard to hand, opponent discards, destroy an artifact. In Legacy, 3 mana for a 2-for-1 is acceptable but not exceptional. It was strong in Czech Pile (2017-2018) where 4-color decks could cast it easily. Currently, it sees occasional play in Grixis and Rakdos builds. The artifact destruction mode is relevant against Chalice, Vial, and Urza's Saga tokens. The main limitation: 3 mana is a lot in Legacy, and more focused 1-2 mana answers often do the job better.

Yes, in specific shells. Retrofitter Foundry costs 1 and can be tutored by Urza's Saga. Its activated abilities create a 1/1 Servo for 3, upgrade a Servo to a 1/1 Thopter (flying) for 1, or upgrade a Thopter to a 4/4 Construct for 1. In combination with Urza's Saga Constructs and free tokens, it provides a repeatable token engine that generates board presence without spending cards. It is best in Azorius Artifacts, D&T (with Stoneforge for equipment synergy), and UW Control with Saga. Not a standalone powerhouse but an excellent Saga tutor target.

Yes, as a sideboard card and occasionally maindeck. Dress Down costs 1U and removes all abilities from all creatures until end of turn, then sacrifices itself. Key interactions: 1) Enables Phyrexian Dreadnought (enters as a 12/12 with no drawback). 2) Blanks Orcish Bowmasters (no trigger), Thalia (no tax), True-Name Nemesis (no protection), Murktide Regent (loses flying, still big). 3) Draws a card (ETB cantrip). The 2-mana instant-speed ability blanking is powerful in creature-heavy matchups. The self-sacrifice means it is a one-turn effect.

Yes, as a powerful sideboard and occasional maindeck enchantment. Back to Basics costs 2U and prevents all nonbasic lands from untapping. Against decks with 15+ nonbasics (most Legacy decks), this effectively locks them out of casting spells. Unlike Blood Moon (which changes land types), Back to Basics just keeps them tapped. The opponent can still tap them for mana before they would untap, but each nonbasic only gets one use then stays tapped forever. Best in mono-blue decks that run many basic Islands. At 3 mana, it is slower than Blood Moon but blue decks can protect it with Force of Will.

Yes, as a sideboard card against artifact strategies. Meltdown costs XR and destroys each artifact with mana value X or less. For X=1 (1R total), it destroys Chalice of the Void (MV 0 on battlefield), Aether Vial (MV 1), Lotus Petal (MV 0), and Mishra's Bauble (MV 0). For X=0 (just R), it destroys all 0-cost artifacts. It is a mass artifact sweeper that scales as needed. It sees play in red sideboards as a flexible answer to artifact-heavy strategies. The sorcery speed is a limitation but the mass destruction compensates.

instruction: Is Force of Vigor the best artifact/enchantment hate in Legacy?
output: It is the best FREE artifact/enchantment hate. For 2GG or by pitching a green card, Force of Vigor destroys up to 2 artifacts and/or enchantments. The free pitch cost is critical: it answers Chalice of the Void (which counters Nature's Claim at MV 1), destroys 2 targets for 1 card (2-for-1 or 3-for-2 including the pitch), and can be cast while tapped out. It is the premium answer to Blood Moon, Chalice, Ensnaring Bridge, The One Ring, and any double-lock board. The downside: requires a green card to pitch, so only green decks play it. Non-green decks use Engineered Explosives or Prismatic Ending instead.

Yes. Null Rod costs 2 and says activated abilities of artifacts cannot be activated. This shuts off: Aether Vial (no creatures), Grindstone (no combo), LED (no mana), Lotus Petal (no mana), Sensei's Divining Top (banned but illustrative), Urza's Saga tokens (no equip), and any artifact mana. It does NOT stop triggered abilities or static abilities, and it does not affect non-artifact permanents. It is devastating against Painter, 8-Cast, Affinity, Mystic Forge, and any deck reliant on artifact activations. Be careful not to play it in decks with your own artifact activations.
